questions.question: The introduction of English education in India was promoted through which of the following documents?
questions.solution: Macaulay’s Minute on Indian Education (1835) was the foundational document for the introduction of English education. Thomas Babington Macaulay argued that Western learning through the medium of English was essential to create a class of people who would assist in British administration. This led to the English Education Act of 1835.

questions.question: When were Bahadur Shah Zafar and his wife Begum Zinat Mahal sent to prison in Rangoon?
questions.solution: After the fall of Delhi during the Revolt of 1857, the last Mughal Emperor Bahadur Shah Zafar was tried for treason. In October 1858, he and his wife, Begum Zinat Mahal, were exiled to Rangoon (modern-day Yangon, Myanmar), where he died in 1862.

questions.question: Which British policy promoted English education to create a loyal class serving British interests in India?
questions.solution: The British Education Policy, specifically following the 1835 Minute, was designed to produce a loyal clerk-class of Indians. By teaching them Western values and the English language, the British hoped to create a buffer group that was 'Indian in blood but English in taste,' ensuring the smooth functioning of the colonial bureaucracy.

questions.question: Who among the following had given the idea to mobilise all the leading Indian political leaders under a common platform called the Indian National Congress?
questions.solution: A.O. Hume, a retired British civil servant, is credited with the idea of creating a national platform for political dialogue. He worked with prominent Indian leaders to organize the first session of the Indian National Congress in December 1885 to act as a 'safety valve' for Indian discontent.

questions.question: What was the main motive of British colonial rule in India?
questions.solution: The fundamental motive of British colonial rule was the economic exploitation of India. India was systematically converted into a supplier of cheap raw materials for British industries and a captive market for finished British machine-made goods, leading to a massive drain of wealth.

questions.question: Which of the following statements regarding the Ryotwari settlement is correct? It was developed by Thomas Munro. The settlement was made directly with the cultivators, not zamindars. It was tested on a small scale in the annexed territories of Tipu. Finding it successful, it was then applied in the Ganga plains.Select the correct combination:
questions.solution:
Ryotwari Settlement

The Ryotwari system was developed by Thomas Munro and Alexander Read. Key facts include:

  • The settlement was made directly with the cultivators (Ryots).
  • It was first tested in the territories taken from Tipu Sultan (Baramahal region).
  • However, it was NOT applied in the Ganga plains; the Mahalwari system was implemented there. The Ryotwari system was dominant in Southern and Western India (Madras and Bombay Presidencies).

questions.question: Pondicherry was captured by the Dutch in 1693 but was returned to the French company by the Treaty of Ryswick in _______.
questions.solution: Pondicherry (Puducherry) changed hands several times during the European power struggle. Captured by the Dutch in 1693 during the Nine Years' War, it was eventually restored to the French in 1699 following the Treaty of Ryswick (signed in 1697).

questions.question: The introduction of English education in India was promoted through which of the following documents?
questions.solution: The introduction of English-medium instruction and Western-style education in India was officially promoted and formalized through Macaulay’s Minute of 1835, which shifted government funding away from Oriental learning toward European sciences and literature.

questions.question: In which year was the first metro line in India, between Esplanade and Bhowanipur in Kolkata, inaugurated—marking the beginning of metro rail services in the country?
questions.solution: India's first metro rail service began in Kolkata. The first stretch, running between Esplanade and Bhowanipur (Netaji Bhavan), was inaugurated on October 24, 1984. It was a landmark achievement in India's urban transport history.

questions.question: Lala Lajpat Rai, Indian revolutionary politician and author was also recognised by which of the following title?
questions.solution: Lala Lajpat Rai was a prominent leader of the extremist wing of the Congress. He was popularly known as Punjab Kesari (the Lion of Punjab) due to his fearless activism and leadership in the anti-British protests in the northern province.

questions.question: The brothers Sidhu and Kanhu were the leaders of which of the following tribal uprisings?
questions.solution: The Santhal Rebellion (1855-56) was a major tribal uprising in the Rajmahal Hills. It was led by the four Murmu brothers: Sidhu, Kanhu, Chand, and Bhairav. They fought against the exploitation of the Damin-i-Koh region by the British and their local agents.

questions.question: In which of the following years was the first thermal power plant of the Calcutta Electric Supply Corporation Limited commissioned?
questions.solution: The Calcutta Electric Supply Corporation (CESC) commissioned India's first thermal power plant at Emambagh Lane in 1899. This marked the beginning of commercial electricity generation in India, making Calcutta the first city in the country to have power.

questions.question: This revolutionary freedom fighter co-founded the Hindustan Socialist Republican Association in 1928. Along with B K Dutt, he threw a bomb in the Central Legislative Assembly in 1929. He was tried and executed at the age of 23, along with revolutionaries Sukhdev Thapar and Shivaram Rajguru. The freedom fighter is:
questions.solution: Bhagat Singh was the iconic revolutionary who transformed the HRA into the HSRA (Hindustan Socialist Republican Association). He was executed on March 23, 1931, in Lahore Jail at the young age of 23, along with his comrades Sukhdev and Rajguru, for the Lahore Conspiracy Case.

questions.question: The Santhal Revolt of 1855-56 was against which groups along with the British officials?
questions.solution: The Santhal Revolt was not just against the British but also against the zamindars (landlords) and moneylenders (collectively called Dikus). These groups had trapped the Santhals in a cycle of debt and had taken over their ancestral lands under British revenue laws.

questions.question: Which Act first recognised the political and administrative functions of the East India Company in India?
questions.solution: The Regulating Act of 1773 was the first time the British Parliament recognized the political and administrative functions of the East India Company. It moved the Company from being a purely commercial entity toward a governing body under parliamentary supervision.

questions.question: How did the Industrial Revolution alter the British economic interests in India?
questions.option_3: From trade to industry and raw material use
questions.solution: Before the Industrial Revolution, the British were primarily traders. After the revolution, their interest shifted toward industry and raw material use. India was forced to export cotton and indigo for British mills and import the finished textiles, leading to the de-industrialization of India.

questions.question: The famous slogan "Swaraj is my birthright and I shall have it " was given by _______.
questions.solution: Bal Gangadhar Tilak, a stalwart of the 'Extremist' faction, gave the slogan "Swaraj is my birthright and I shall have it!" to instill a sense of pride and urgency in the Indian masses to achieve complete self-rule.

questions.question: In which of the following years was Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar born, who is remembered for his efforts in widow remarriage and the abolition of polygamy in Hindu society?
questions.solution: Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar, the great polymath and reformer, was born in 1820 in Bengal. He is best known for his scholarly work and tireless campaigning that led to the passage of the Hindu Widows' Remarriage Act of 1856.

questions.question: Which leader founded the Servants of India Society to train Indians for national service and political awareness?
questions.solution: Gopal Krishna Gokhale founded the Servants of India Society in 1905. Its objective was to train 'national missionaries' for the service of India and to promote social and economic interests through constitutional means.

questions.question: Colonial policies led to the commercialisation of agriculture. Which of the following crops was encouraged for export during British rule?
questions.solution: Under colonial rule, agriculture was commercialized to serve the British textile industry. Indigo was the most prominent crop encouraged (and often forced) for export, as it was used as a dye in England. This often led to farmer distress, sparking events like the Indigo Revolt.

questions.question: After a hundred years of conquest and administration, the English East India Company faced a massive uprising that took place in _________.
questions.solution: In 1857, exactly 100 years after the Battle of Plassey (1757), the English East India Company faced the Great Revolt (Sepoy Mutiny). It started in Meerut and spread across North and Central India, nearly toppling British rule.

questions.question: Andhra Pradesh was formed in 1953 by separating Telugu-speaking areas from which of the following states?
questions.solution: In 1953, Andhra Pradesh was carved out of the Madras State (Presidency) following the death of Potti Sreeramulu. This was the first state in India to be created on a linguistic basis, specifically for Telugu-speaking people.

questions.question: During the colonial period in India, the Vernacular Press Act (1878) was introduced by _____.
questions.solution: The Vernacular Press Act of 1878, also known as the 'Gagging Act,' was introduced by Lord Lytton. It was designed to suppress the local language newspapers that were critical of British policies, particularly during the Second Anglo-Afghan War.

questions.question: Pick the INCORRECT statement regarding the life of Birsa Munda.
questions.option_1: He had joined the local missionary school.
questions.option_2: He urged people to destroy the Europeans.
questions.option_3: He advised his people to bring back the Golden Age of Mundas.
questions.option_4: Birsa was born in the forests of Madhya Pradesh.
questions.solution:
Birsa Munda: Incorrect Fact

The incorrect statement is that Birsa was born in the forests of Madhya Pradesh. In reality, Birsa Munda was born in Ulihatu (Chotanagpur region), which is in present-day Jharkhand. He was the leader of the 'Ulgulan' or Great Tumult.

questions.question: Which of the following years saw the foundation of the Home Rule League and its phenomenal success?
questions.solution: The year 1916 saw the foundation of two Home Rule Leagues: one by Bal Gangadhar Tilak (in April) and another by Annie Besant (in September). The movement was highly successful in popularizing the demand for self-government within the British Empire.
